full_text: um let's think about something for a moment what makes a good pad sound is it the gentle rise and fall of a filter is it the sweeping modulation of the waveforms is it the way the waves guide us inward to capture and extend a tangled vignette of memories into an eternal present is it the way the sound carries us beyond our own senses to explore the recesses of our subjective consciousness to answer the big questions what is life what is our art but a collective refraction of our own ultimately fleeting existence and expression do people really actually like taco bell nacho fries but more importantly how do we learn to employ the indescribably personal summation of our life and experiences through sound without someone first telling us to punch the like button and hit the bell to join their notification squad before we dive into another six serum tutorial without first talking about today's sponsor dollar shave shadow vpn as a youtuber described by one user as having no idea what he's doing i feel fully qualified to say that today i'm going to show you how it's done okay howdy doody buckaroonies as mentioned in that spicy intro we're going to be talking about pads today and rather than doing a specific sound i wanted to do more of a conceptual breakdown of my approach to designing pad sounds in this video we're going to be talking about pad sounds obviously but a lot of these ideas can be applied to just about anything because again we're going to be painting in kind of big broad strokes i'm going to be using a bunch of different plugins to do this here today but keep in mind that literally everything i'm about to show you can be done in any daw with stock plugins i can't name a daw offhand that couldn't do what i'm about to show you so feel free to follow along with whatever tools you would like with that remember that life is a highway and there is really no wrong answer to any of this this is just kind of my approach and theory behind it so feel free to modify these techniques and do whatever you want and if you come up with something cool feel free to share it with me as you probably saw it's a nice rainy gloomy day outside so i figured today would be a great time to film a tutorial about this kind of stuff this is the patch we're going to be talking about today but again we're not really talking settings and recreating i wanted to talk just about the overall concept to design interesting pads that are super cool and unique and not just like a saw with a low pass that goes blue or whatever for this sound today i'm going to be using dune 3 this is one of my favorite synths and i haven't shown it on the channel here in a while i'm not really sure why but why i'm going to be using this is because it's multi-timbral or meaning i can play multiple patches or layers at the same time however you don't need a synth that's multi-timbral to do this you could just use a couple instances of your favorite synth stack together group them together and then apply all the post processing to that group so this patch is made up of three layers and let's talk about the how and the why before we dive into some of the deeper details of the synth patch design so this is the first layer cool just kind of a nice dark lower mid-rangey sort of thing let's take a listen to layer two sort of a brighter area upper mid ranges sort of thing and then layer three very subtle hint of modulated noise to add some space and dimension to things once we put all three together what do we get lovely so first up one of the things i would like to instill in your mind when it comes to designing sounds in general is that often the most interesting things are something that is greater than the sum of its parts we're building up a sound from a couple of different layers and each of these layers is contributing some kind of specific aspect that complements the other parts of this whether this is designing a kick drum or a base or a pad or a lead whatever it may be usually there's something good to be done with layering in order to enhance the aspects of a sound you like or build on top of them rather than trying to make one sound that does it all more specific to pad design though is the idea of modulation and movement if i hold a note here for a sort of extended period think about what you're hearing and mind you i'm not touching anything i'm just holding some notes do you hear how the sound kind of grows and evolves and changes and never really is static that is a huge part of interesting sound design i think i mean there's sounds that work really well as static sounds maybe like an 808 being a prime example but when it comes to things like leads or some bases or pads especially we want this constant sense of evolution and movement and unpredictability because oftentimes the role of a pad is to pad out the arrangement we're going to have a very broad spectrum sound that's going to fill in a bunch of stuff and we're going to play long droning notes and it's often going to feed into big delays or washy reverbs or things like that and the thing with that being if it's too static it sounds boring and it just sounds like going on the whole time underneath the mix rather than something interesting and evolving that adds to the mood and the overall sonic palette and i guess mental imagery that your track is going to conjure up so modulation is really key for this kind of stuff more specifically to the synthesizer section here if we take a look at this first layer we'll see that we have wave table oscillators and i find that wave table synths are really kind of the go-to for pads at least for me a lot of the time wave sequencing synths are another really great thing as well because they provide a very similar benefit a lot of the times when we listen to older pad sounds you know your kind of classic detune saws or like a pulse mod square pad or something you know they sound a little basic and what the offerings of wave table synthesizers bring a lot of cool ideas to the table we have this constant movement and evolution we can sweep through the waveforms we can do lots of different things and with modern synthesizers we can blend lots and lots and lots of different things together and get these really really crazy results so i've got oscillator one here as a wave table i'm modulating its position if we go into the mod matrix i've got this coming from the lfo so let's hop in there as you can see here i've got a fair amount of modulation going on in this synthesizer so lfo1 is targeting oscillator 1 and 2's wave table position if we hop over to voice two we've got a very similar story we've got wave table here we've got a wave table here we're moving that around as well using these lfos we could blend in the second oscillator a bit we've got lfo3 targeting the filter cutoff of all three voices but i've got different filters which we'll talk about momentarily we've got the msegs as well i've used these to create some basic lfos just kind of a triangle lfo using that to target the global pitch of individual layers and that way we just get this constant moving flowing evolving thing when it comes to the individual layers here one of the big things i'm trying to do as mentioned is design complementary things that are going to fill out the spectrum and be something bigger so on the first layer as mentioned we have this kind of low rumbling grumbling mid-rangeish thing now in order to complement that because this is using a low pass filter with a bit of resonance and drive i wanted to add a bit more upper mids and air so that's where the second layer comes in if we take a look at this this is a band pass with high resonance some filter envelope the first layer doesn't have any envelope to the filter it's just being moved by the lfo so this is receiving similar yet different modulation so i've got a very long filter envelope here that closes back off long attack decay and release phases similarly on the first layer the amp envelope very long long long stuff i have a different amp envelope here on the second layer so that these layers aren't really too similar there's different movements but similar things and that's an important concept this is a pretty restricted filter so it's 24 decibel per octave band pass we're moving this around and that's going to create this kind of mid-range to high-end layer and layering those together if we bring in our first layer underneath we've got something that feels like one sound it's very unified but it's two very different ideas that come together and form something that is bigger than the sum of its parts for a final bit of enhancement i usually like to add some kind of noise or something to my synth pad so if we go into layer three here this oscillator is actually entirely disabled but i have the slightest hint of noise i think only about six percent and that is being
